chunks.text: First Information Report — Case No. 214/2024 Police Station: T. Nagar | District: Chennai | State: Tamil Nadu Date of FIR: 14 March 2024 | Time: 10:40 hrs 1. Complainant Details Name: Rajesh Kumar Iyer, S/o Krishnan Iyer, Age 42, Occupation: Proprietor, M/s Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd, No. 18 Usman Road, T. Nagar, Chennai - 600017. 2. Accused Name: Suresh Babu Menon, S/o Gopalakrishna Menon, Age 37, Occupation: Business Partner, residing at Flat 4B, Kamala Towers, Anna Nagar, Chennai - 600040. 3. Sections of Law Offence registered under Section 420 (Cheating), Section 406 (Criminal Breach of Trust), and Section 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) of the Indian Penal Code, 1860. 4. Brief Facts of the Case The complainant states that on 3 January 2024, he entered into a partnership agreement with the accused for the supply of textile machinery, under which the accused was to deposit a sum of Rs. 12,50,000 (Rupees Twelve Lakh Fifty Thousand only) as working capital into the joint business account maintained at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch, Account No. XXXXXX4471. The complainant further states that as per the signed Memorandum of Understanding dated 3 January 2024, the accused was required to deposit the amount within 15 days, i.e. on or before 18 January 2024. The complainant alleges that despite repeated reminders on 20 January, 5 February, and 28 February 2024, the accused failed to deposit the said amount and instead withdrew Rs. 3,40,000 from the joint account on 22 January 2024 without the complainant's consent. The complainant states he personally visited the accused's residence on 1 March 2024 to discuss the matter, at which time the accused allegedly admitted verbally to having used the funds for personal purposes and promised repayment within a week. No repayment was made. 5. Witnesses Cited (i) Mr. Anand Prakash, Accountant, Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd (ii) Ms. Divya Shankar, Bank Relationship Manager, Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch 6. Documents Enclosed 1. Memorandum of Understanding dated 3 January 2024 (Annexure A) 2. Bank statement of joint account for period 1 Jan 2024 to 10 Mar 2024 (Annexure B)
3. WhatsApp chat transcript between complainant and accused, 20 Jan - 1 Mar 2024 (Annexure C)

Witness Statement — Recorded under Section 161 CrPC Witness: Anand Prakash, Age 29, Accountant, Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd Recorded on: 18 March 2024 | Investigating Officer: Sub-Inspector R. Vignesh Statement I have been employed as accountant with Iyer Textiles Pvt Ltd since June 2021. I handle the day-to-day bookkeeping and bank reconciliation for the firm, including the joint account opened with Mr. Suresh Babu Menon at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch. I state that the joint account was opened on 3 January 2024 with an initial deposit of Rs. 50,000 made by the complainant, Mr. Rajesh Kumar Iyer. I confirm that as per the account statements I reviewed, Mr. Suresh Babu Menon did not deposit the agreed sum of Rs. 12,50,000 at any point between January and March 2024. I further state that on 22 January 2024, a withdrawal of Rs. 3,40,000 was made from the joint account. To the best of my knowledge, this withdrawal was authorised by both signatories, since the account required joint signatures for any withdrawal above Rs. 1,00,000. I did not personally witness who initiated the withdrawal request at the bank counter. I recall that Mr. Iyer informed me around 25 January 2024, not in March as he may have stated elsewhere, that he suspected something was wrong with the account, and asked me to prepare a detailed reconciliation, which I completed by 2 February 2024. I am aware of at least two reminder emails sent to Mr. Menon, dated 20 January and 5 February 2024. I am not aware of any reminder sent on 28 February 2024 as I did not draft or send one on that date.

Witness Statement — Recorded under Section 161 CrPC Witness: Divya Shankar, Age 34, Relationship Manager, Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch Recorded on: 20 March 2024 | Investigating Officer: Sub-Inspector R. Vignesh Statement I am employed as Relationship Manager at Indian Overseas Bank, T. Nagar Branch, since 2018. I handled the opening of the joint current account bearing number ending 4471 in the names of Mr. Rajesh Kumar Iyer and Mr. Suresh Babu Menon. As per our bank records, the account was opened on 3 January 2024. The mandate on file specifies 'Either or Survivor' operation, meaning either signatory could operate the account independently, including withdrawals of any amount, without requiring the co-signature of the other party. I confirm that a withdrawal of Rs. 3,40,000 was made on 22 January 2024 via a cheque signed solely by Mr. Suresh Babu Menon. I state clearly that no joint signature was required or obtained for this transaction, as the account mandate did not mandate dual authorisation at any amount. I further confirm from our records that no deposit of Rs. 12,50,000, or any amount close to it, was ever credited to this account by Mr. Menon between January and March 2024. The only credit entries in this period were the initial Rs. 50,000 deposit on 3 January 2024 and a subsequent Rs. 15,000 deposit on 10 February 2024, source unspecified in our records.

Evidence & Exhibit Log Case No. 214/2024 | Compiled by: Investigating Officer, T. Nagar Police Station Exhibit A — Memorandum of Understanding (3 January 2024) Clause 4 of the MoU states: 'Party B (Suresh Babu Menon) shall deposit Rs. 12,50,000 into the joint operating account within 15 days of execution of this agreement.' Clause 7 states the account shall operate on an 'Either or Survivor' basis for transactions up to Rs. 2,00,000 only, and shall require joint signature for any withdrawal exceeding Rs. 2,00,000 — this appears to conflict with the bank's own mandate record described in Exhibit C. Exhibit B — Bank Statement (1 Jan 2024 to 10 Mar 2024) Opening balance: Rs. 0. Credit of Rs. 50,000 on 3 Jan 2024. Credit of Rs. 15,000 on 10 Feb 2024. Debit of Rs. 3,40,000 on 22 Jan 2024, cheque no. 002841, single signatory: S. B. Menon. Closing balance as of 10 Mar 2024: Rs. -2,75,000 (overdraft). Exhibit C — Bank Account Mandate Form Operating instructions field marked: 'Either or Survivor — No Limit'. Signed by both parties on 3 January 2024. This directly contradicts Clause 7 of the MoU (Exhibit A), which purports to cap single-signatory withdrawals at Rs. 2,00,000. Exhibit D — WhatsApp Transcript (20 Jan - 1 Mar 2024) 20 Jan 2024, 11:02 - Rajesh Iyer: 'Suresh, the deposit was due two days ago, please confirm status.' 20 Jan 2024, 18:45 - Suresh Menon: 'Processing it this week, minor delay on my end.' 5 Feb 2024, 09:15 - Rajesh Iyer: 'Still no deposit and I see a large withdrawal instead. Explain.' 5 Feb 2024, 20:03 - Suresh Menon: 'I needed it for an emergency, will return by month end.' 1 Mar 2024, 16:30 - Rajesh Iyer: 'You have not returned anything. I am going to the police.' 1 Mar 2024, 16:52 - Suresh Menon: 'Do what you want, the account was mine to operate.' Note on Discrepancies Investigating officer flags two items for legal review: (i) the conflicting withdrawal-limit clauses between the MoU and the bank mandate form; and (ii) a discrepancy in witness recollection regarding the date the complainant first raised concerns internally (25 January per Anand Prakash's statement) versus the complainant's own FIR narrative, which does not mention this internal date at all and instead centres on the 1 March 2024 confrontation.

analysis.summary:
## Executive Summary
The case involves a complaint filed by Rajesh Kumar Iyer against Suresh Babu Menon for cheating, criminal breach of trust, and criminal conspiracy under the Indian Penal Code, 1860. The dispute arises from a partnership agreement where Menon was supposed to deposit Rs. 12,50,000 into a joint business account but failed to do so and instead withdrew Rs. 3,40,000 without Iyer's consent. The key parties include the complainant, the accused, and witnesses such as Anand Prakash and Divya Shankar. The core legal dispute revolves around the interpretation of the partnership agreement, the bank account mandate, and the actions of the accused.

## Key Statutory Sections & Case Law
- Section 420 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860: Applies as the complainant alleges that the accused cheated him by not depositing the agreed amount and instead withdrawing a significant sum without consent.
- Section 406 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860: Relevant as the complainant claims that the accused committed a criminal breach of trust by misusing the funds in the joint account.
- Section 120B of the Indian Penal Code, 1860: Applicable as the complainant accuses the accused of criminal conspiracy in relation to the partnership agreement and the misuse of funds.
- Section 161 of the Criminal Procedure Code, 1973: Applies as witness statements were recorded under this section.

## Legal Web Leads
The legal web search did not yield any usable results for this query. Therefore, the analysis relies solely on the provided PDF excerpts.

## Contradictions & Inconsistencies
1. The Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) states that the account shall operate on an 'Either or Survivor' basis for transactions up to Rs. 2,00,000 only, and shall require joint signature for any withdrawal exceeding Rs. 2,00,000 (Exhibit A, Clause 7), but the bank mandate form specifies 'Either or Survivor — No Limit' (Exhibit C), allowing either party to withdraw any amount without the other's signature.
2. The complainant's FIR states that he suspected something was wrong with the account and mentions a confrontation on 1 March 2024, but Anand Prakash's witness statement mentions that the complainant informed him of his suspicions around 25 January 2024 (Excerpt 2), indicating a discrepancy in the timeline of when the complainant first raised concerns.
3. The bank statement (Exhibit B) shows a debit of Rs. 3,40,000 on 22 January 2024 with a single signatory (S. B. Menon), but Anand Prakash initially states that withdrawals above Rs. 1,00,000 required joint signatures (Excerpt 2), which contradicts the bank's mandate and the actual transaction record.
4. Divya Shankar's statement confirms that the account mandate allowed either signatory to operate the account independently, including withdrawals of any amount (Excerpt 4), which supports the bank's records but contradicts the initial statement by Anand Prakash regarding the requirement for joint signatures for large withdrawals.

## Missing Facts & Evidence Gaps
- The exact terms and conditions of the partnership agreement beyond the mentioned clauses in the MoU.
- Detailed financial records of the accused to ascertain the use of the withdrawn Rs. 3,40,000.
- Direct evidence of the accused's admission of using the funds for personal purposes, as alleged by the complainant.
- Clarification on the discrepancy in the account mandate and the MoU regarding the 'Either or Survivor' clause and its implications.

## Strategic Insights & Next Steps
- The prosecution should focus on establishing the intent of the accused to cheat and breach trust, highlighting the discrepancies in the accused's actions and statements.
- The defense should explore the contradictions in the witnesses' statements and the documents (MoU vs. bank mandate) to argue against the charges of cheating and criminal breach of trust.
- Investigate further into the financial transactions of the accused to trace the use of the withdrawn amount and to verify the claim of using it for personal purposes.
- Consider obtaining expert opinion on the legal implications of the 'Either or Survivor' clause in the context of the partnership agreement and the bank account mandate.
